text: To develop catalysts that efficiently make ammonia under catalytic conditions from N2, a detailed understanding of the mechanisms and events that determine rates and ammonea yields and selectivity is required. To this end, we have employed Samarium Diiodide (SmI2) in conjunction with water as a low pressure proton source along with combinations of transition metal complexes and PCP ligands. Catalyst kinetics are detailed and reveal rate-determining steps in the catalytic cycle that depend on catalyst concentration. Catalyst dimerization limits activity at low catalyst concentration while the proton-coupled electron transfer event that creates the N-H bond on the Mo imide is rate determining at high catalyst concentration. Based on this finding, a detailed systematic investigation involving DFT studies was carried out to optimize the ligand structure and facilitate a rapid reaction that affords high NH3 yields and selectivity. A new set of catalysts derived from varying ligand core PCP ligands with different sets of 5,6 ring substitutions derived from 1,2,4-triazole and benzene cores, derived from various set of electron rich/electron deficient groups provided the key to optimizing the catalytic properties of the new family of molybdenum trichloride catalysts. Specifically, the trifluoromethyl substituted analogue that we employed as an active catalyst precursor affords a turnover number of 60,000 equivalents of NH3 catalyst and TOF of 800 equivalents of NH3 per minute.
